these fields often involve classifying, clustering, or comparing
1
v subspaces, requiring the use of appropriate similarity measures
3 or kernel functions. However, standard Euclidean methods fail
3 due to the non-Euclidean nature of Grassmannians [7], requir-

1
Kernels on Grassmannians have been extensively studied,
2
. with notable examples  including the projection kernel and
4
the Binet-Cauchy kernel [17]. These kernels provide means
0
to embed subspaces into a reproducing kernel Hilbert space
5
2 (RKHS), allowing the application of powerful classification
:
algorithms such as support vector machines (SVMs). However,
v
i the large storage cost required to store the kernel matrix often
X makes such methods impractical in large datasets.
r
In  this work, we propose a sketching mechanism that
a
approximates subspace similarity by mapping projection ma-
trices into a feature space through random embeddings.  In-
spired by the methodology of Rahimi and Recht  [13] also
described in [18], our approach provides an alternative way
to apply Grassmannian kernels  in  large-scale  settings by
avoiding explicit computation of  the Gram matrix which is
beneficial when working with large datasets or when storing
and computing full kernel matrices becomes infeasible.
Our main contributions are as follows. We propose three
kernel approximation schemes for Grassmannian manifolds
based on  random projections,  specifically using  rank-one
projections (ROP). Two of  these schemes involve binarised
embeddings, which drastically reduce both memory require-

rassmannian Kernels
Laurent Jacques
M, UCLouvain, Belgium
acques}@uclouvain.be
ments and computational cost by replacing real-valued inner
products with simple binary operations. We then analyse the
expected behaviour of  these approximations  in the special
case of G(1, n), showing closed-form dependence on principal
angles, and we extend key insights  to the general G(k, n)
setting. Finally we provide experimental validation on syn-
thetic and real-world datasets, demonstrating that even fully
binarised sketches can match the classification accuracy of
exact Grassmannian kernels for sufficiently large sketches.
The remainder of  the paper  is structured as follows:  In
Sec. II, we provide background on Grassmannian manifolds
and existing kernel methods. Sec. III introduces our sketching-
based approximation, detailing its mathematical formulation
and properties. Sec.  IV presents experimental  results,  and
Sec. V discusses applications and future work.

a) Grassmannian manifolds: The Grassmannian mani-
fold G(k, n) is the set of all k-dimensional linear subspaces
Rn
of  . These manifolds appear naturally in various fields like
subspace clustering, dimensionality reduction, and signal pro-
cessing, where data can be represented by subspaces instead
of vectors.
An element of G(k, n) is commonly represented by an n×k
⊤
matrix U with orthonormal columns, satisfying U U = Ik,
where Ik  is the k × k identity matrix. However, since the
choice of basis is not unique, an equivalent representation is
⊤
given by the projection matrix: P = UU  , where P is an
2
n × n symmetric and idempotent matrix, satisfying P = P .
This formulation ensures that comparisons between elements
of G(k, n) are invariant to basis choices.
The Grassmannian manifold is a Riemannian manifold with
a well-defined geometric structure, making it a natural setting
for tasks requiring subspace comparisons. It has applications
in areas such as image recognition, motion segmentation, and
dictionary learning.
b) Principal angles and distance metrics: To measure
the similarity between two subspaces P ,Q ∈ G(k, n), we
use principal angles θ1, . . . , θk, which quantify the degree of
alignment between the subspaces. These angles are obtained
⊤
from the  singular  value decomposition  (SVD), P Q =
⊤
V 1ΣV 2 , where Σ = diag(cos θ1, . . . , cos θk) contains the
cosines of the principal angles.
Several distance metrics on G(k, n) are derived from these
angles, for example the geodesic distance,

A. Random projections for Grassmannian kernels
Random projections have been widely used as an efficient
method to simplify calculations in kernel methods, particularly
in the context of large-scale learning problems. Inspired by the
original work of Rahimi and Recht [13], we use random pro-
jection techniques to the setting of Grassmannian manifolds.
Given a subspace P ∈ G(k, n), our goal is to construct a
sketch that preserves principal angle-based similarity. Instead
of working directly with projection matrices, we define a
structured embedding using rank-one random projections.

Rn
More formally,  let ai, bi ∈  be independent standard
Gaussian vectors for i = 1, . . . ,m. We define the random
Rm
projection operator R : G(k, n) →  as
⊤  m
R(P) = (⟨aibi , P⟩)i=1.  (3)
Each entry of the sketched representation R(P) corresponds
⊤
to an inner product between the rank one matrix aibi  and the
subspace, effectively capturing information about the structure
of P . As we shall see later, the parameter m can be used to
control the quality of the random projection. As m increases,
the approximation error of the embedding will decrease.
B. Three kernel approximation mechanisms
We introduce three variations of the kernel approximation
mechanism, each offering different trade-offs between approx-
imation accuracy and computational  efficiency. We denote
kernel functions using κi and their approximations using κ˜i.
1) Non-binarised  approximation:  The  first  and most
straightforward approach is to retain the real-valued sketched
vector R(P). The resulting kernel approximation is given by:
κ˜1(P ,Q) = ⟨R(P),R(Q)⟩.  (4)
This method approximates the projection kernel  (2).  It has
the highest fidelity to the original subspace similarity but still
requires storage of continuous values.
2) Semi-binarised approximation:  This  approach  is  in-
spired by the asymmetric sketching mechanism proposed in
[14], where an asymmetric sketching mechanism is used for
data storage and incoming queries. Specifically, we sketch
the dataset using a binarised embedding S  :  G(k, n) →
Rm
: P (cid:55)→ sign(R(P)), where the sign operator is applied
componentwise to the sketched vector R(P), and compute
inner products with non-binarised embeddings of query points,
i.e., we define the kernel
κ˜2(P ,Q) = ⟨S(P),R(Q)⟩.  (5)
Although κ˜2 is not a valid kernel  in the strict sense (i.e.,  it
1
is not symmetric ),  it  is a kernel  in expectation, meaning it
converges to a well-defined limit as m→∞.
This semi-binarised design is particularly attractive in asym-
metrical  scenarios  such as  low-bandwidth settings, where
sensors  transmit  compact binary sketches  to a server  that
retains higher precision data (described in details in [14]). It
enables efficient scalar product computation while reducing
communication and storage costs on the sensor or server side.
3) Fully binarised approximation:  In the most  extreme
case, we enforce full binarisation of  the sketch with corre-
sponding kernel approximation given by:
κ˜3(P ,Q) = ⟨S(P), S(Q)⟩.  (6)
While this approach significantly reduces memory the result-
ing kernel (obtained in expectation) is not easy to characterise
as we will explain.
1
Note that one can still use a symmetric kernel based on R and S by
′
considering κ˜2(x, y) := (⟨R(x), S(y)⟩ + ⟨S(x),R(y)⟩)/2.

C. Properties of the Sketched Kernels
Each of  the proposed kernels exhibits a fundamental de-
pendence on the principal angles between subspaces. For the
case of G(1, n), we can derive closed-form expressions for the
expected kernel values in terms of the single principal angle.
Positive-definiteness and symmetry: Standard kernel  func-
tions are required to be positive definite and symmetric [15].
The first two kernel functions, k1 and k2 are provably positive-
definite because, as we will see, they can both be expressed by
a scalar product. For k3 it is less immediate, but experimentally
it seems that it retains this property when k = 1.

Proposition 3. For P ,Q ∈ G(k, n), with principal angles
θ1, . . . , θk between them, κ3(P ,Q) = f(θ1, . . . , θk) for some
function f.  In other words κ3(P ,Q) only depends on the
principal angles between P and Q.

E. Benefits of approximation
As observed in [13], [10] and [18], the primary advantage
of approximating kernel functions via random features is not
necessarily a reduction in computational complexity or dataset
size, but rather circumventing the storage of a Gram matrix
which becomes infeasible when the number of instances to.

We evaluate the proposed kernel approximations on both
synthetic  and  real-world data. Our  primary classification
method is support vector machines (SVMs),  trained either
with a precomputed Gram matrix or directly in the sketched
feature space, eliminating the need for computing and storing
the Gram matrix. We also validate the approximation quality
of κ˜2 using a nearest subspace classifier.
A. Experiments on Artificial Subspaces
R1024
Synthetic subspaces of dimension 9 in  (in G(9, 1024))
are generated by sampling random vectors and orthonormalis-
3
ing  them with QR decomposition. Test subspaces are obtained
by perturbing the training ones with additive noise.
We train SVM classifiers in two settings: (i) using the exact
kernel κ1 with a precomputed Gram matrix, and (ii) using its
approximations κ˜1 and κ˜3 without Gram computation, operat-
ing directly in the sketched feature space. This demonstrates
the possibility of removing the Gram matrix in kernel methods
on Grassmannian. Despite lacking a theoretical expectation, κ˜3
also achieves strong classification performance.
To assess  the quality of κ˜2, we use a nearest  subspace
classifier, which assigns test subspaces to the class with the
highest similarity. For m ≥ 5000, the classification accuracy
using κ˜2  is  indistinguishable from that obtained with κ2,
confirming its validity as an approximation.
To account  for randomness in the sketching process, we
average results over 10 independent  trials, and the standard
deviations are reported in Fig. 1(left). For sufficiently large
m, κ˜1 and κ˜3 match the performance of κ1, with κ˜3 requiring
more features but offering storage benefits due to its binarisa-
tion.
3
Orthonormalisation incurs an additional computational cost but is in line
with existing methods such as [1].

B. Experiments on ETH-80 Dataset
We apply the same methodology to the ETH-80 dataset [9]
[3], which consists of images from 8 object categories (apples,
pears, cows, . . . ) captured from multiple angles. Following [8]
where the authors explain that objects pictured from different
Rn
angles can be modelled by a 9-dimensional subspace of  ,
each object class is modelled as a 9-dimensional subspace in
Rn
. Training subspaces are extracted from greyscale images
resized to n = 32 × 32, and classification is performed on
subspaces constructed from unseen images. As in the synthetic
case, we compare SVM classifiers trained with κ1, κ˜1, and
κ˜3, while using nearest subspace classification to validate κ˜2.
Fig. 1(right) shows that both κ˜1 and κ˜3 perform well both
converging to κ1’s accuracy- at large m. The nearest subspace
3
classifier confirms that for m ≥ 10 , κ˜2 achieves the same
classification accuracy as κ2.
One should keep in mind that  through the binarisation
process we reduce the storage by a factor of 64 compared to
storing in double-precision as is the case in most computers,
making our approach more efficient even when m seems large.

We have shown that Grassmannian kernels can be approx-
imated using random features, enabling classification without
computing or storing a Gram matrix. Through experiments
on synthetic and real-world data, we demonstrated that  the
approximations κ˜1 and κ˜3 achieve similar performances to κ1
when m is large, with κ˜3 offering additional storage benefits
due to its binarisation. We also validated the approximation
quality of κ˜2 using another classifier,  showing that  it can
reproduce the classification results of κ2 for large m. While
κ˜2 doesn’t provide computational advantages in SVM classi-
fication, it confirms the validity of the sketching process.
Future work directions include the theoretical analysis of
κ˜3 beyond the k = 1 case, as its strong empirical perfor-
mance suggests potential for further study. Another direction
is developing direct subspace construction from sketches,  to
allow classification to be performed without explicitly forming
projection matrices, further reducing computational power.
